description: §39.5 real DDL UNIQUE constraint enforced by the engine (RUNTIME, sqlEngine:"real"). The <schema> declares `email: text unique`; Fork B derives the CREATE TABLE (with the UNIQUE constraint) from that block via schema-differ generateCreateTable. tryAdd runs `INSERT OR IGNORE INTO members (email) VALUES (${email})`.run() with a duplicate email; the real engine's UNIQUE constraint makes OR IGNORE skip the row. memberCount then reads COUNT(*) → {n:1} (unchanged). The stub has no table and no constraint — its COUNT `.get()` returns the raw seed row — so {n:1} proves the real DDL constraint fired.
rationale: §39.5: a `unique` column constraint lowers to SQL `UNIQUE` in the generated DDL (schema-differ generateCreateTable — the exact helper protect-analyzer uses). Fork B derive-from-<schema>: the constraint is REAL only because the DDL comes from the case's own schema. `INSERT OR IGNORE` observes the constraint WITHOUT the (currently unimplemented) server-side SqlError→::ConstraintViolation envelope: a duplicate is silently skipped, so the count stays 1. NOTE — the §8.7 error-surface form (a throwing constraint violation caught by an `!{}` `::ConstraintViolation` arm) is BLOCKED: the compiler emits the client-side envelope dispatch but NO server-side SqlError classification, so the throw never becomes a scrml error. This case pins the ENGINE-level enforcement; the error-surface is a separate compiler gap (surfaced in the dispatch report). FK enforcement is out of scope (SQLite foreign_keys pragma is OFF by default — matching deploy).
